BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo).   <narrative>The slot machine uses a centralized state management framework provided by astarte-framework. CommonState defines standard game state constants (IDLE, SPIN, WAIT_RES, etc.) imported from the framework's utilities module, while stateManager is a singleton imported from the framework's Managers module that controls state transitions. GameView initializes the state machine by creating concrete state objects (like WaitResState) and mapping them to CommonState enum values. Developer tools like ReelDevTool import stateManager directly to inspect or manipulate game state for testing purposes. This separation between state definitions (CommonState), state controller (stateManager), and state implementations (WaitResState, etc.) follows a clean architecture pattern.</narrative>

<observation>
  <type>change</type>
  <title>Added CommonState import to ReelDevTool</title>
  <subtitle>Imported CommonState enum to enable state checking in developer tool</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>ReelDevTool.ts now imports CommonState from "db://astarte-framework/utilis"</fact>
    <fact>CommonState import added alongside existing stateManager import</fact>
    <fact>Import enables ReelDevTool to check current game state before triggering test spins</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Added CommonState enum import to ReelDevTool.ts to enable state checking functionality. This allows the developer tool to verify the game is in the correct state (e.g., IDLE) before triggering test operations. The import is placed next to the existing stateManager import, keeping state-related imports grouped together. This addition supports the pending functionality to guard against triggering spins when the game is not ready or is in an inappropriate state.</narrative>

content.thinking:
These are three consecutive edits to ReelDevTool.ts that implement a comprehensive fix for premature spin triggering. Let me combine them into one observation:

1. Added CommonState import
2. Added IsGameReady() helper method that checks both IsLoadingComplete AND stateManager.Current() === CommonState.IDLE
3. Updated the update() loop to use IsGameReady() instead of just IsLoadingComplete
4. Updated TestStandardSpin() to use IsGameReady() and updated the log message

This is a significant bugfix that addresses a race condition where the devtool could try to spin before the game state machine was ready (even if the UI was built).

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Fixed premature spin trigger in ReelDevTool by checking game state readiness</title>
  <subtitle>DevTool now waits for both UI completion and IDLE state before allowing spins</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>ReelDevTool.IsGameReady() method checks both m_slotReel.IsLoadingComplete and stateManager.Current() === CommonState.IDLE</fact>
    <fact>TestStandardSpin() guards against premature triggers by storing request in m_pendingTestPlate when game not ready</fact>
    <fact>update() loop executes pending test request automatically once IsGameReady() returns true</fact>
    <fact>Previous implementation only checked IsLoadingComplete, missing state machine readiness</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed race condition where ReelDevTool could trigger spins before the game state machine entered IDLE state. The previous implementation only verified that the slot reel UI was built (IsLoadingComplete) but didn't account for the game still being in LOGIN state or playing opening animations. The new IsGameReady() method combines two conditions: (1) slot reel UI built, and (2) state machine in IDLE state. When either condition fails, the spin request is stored in m_pendingTestPlate and automatically executed once both conditions are met. Log messages updated to reflect the dual requirement. This prevents "spinning during login" errors and ensures the game is truly ready to accept spin commands.</narrative>
